notes.note: Sponsio: a formal Roman procedural wager by which a disputed legal point was framed for trial, each party staking a sum on the truth of a contested assertion. Here it tests whether Turpilia's will gave Q. Caepio valid title to her estate.

notes.anchor: the praetor Q. Caepio has given me possession
notes.note: The praetor 'Q. Caepio' is almost certainly M. Junius Brutus, who used the name Q. Servilius Caepio Brutus after adoption and was urban praetor in 44 BC; the wording quotes his edict granting bonorum possessio (praetorian possession of an estate).

notes.anchor: the capacity to make a will
notes.note: Factio testamenti: the legal capacity to make (or take under) a valid will. Servius Sulpicius held that tablets drawn up by someone lacking this capacity could not count as a testament at all.
Testa is the cognomen of the young jurist C. Trebatius Testa, the recipient; Cicero addresses him familiarly by it throughout the cluster.
